original_hypothesis.title: PAYEE Orders Have Zero On-Chain Footprint — Assets Unrecoverable by Protocol
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: In Create._rentFromZone, when the order type is PAYEE, the entire storage recording block is skipped. No order hash is stored in Storage.orders, no rental asset updates are recorded, no ERC20 deposit increments occur, and no hooks are processed. PAYEE orders exist only as Seaport order events. If the paired PAY order fails, is never created, or is stopped independently, the PAYEE-side assets (NFTs and ERC20s transferred via Seaport considerations) have no protocol-level tracking and cannot be stopped/reclaimed through the protocol's mechanisms.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The _rentFromZone function has an explicit condition: if (payload.metadata.orderType.isBaseOrder() || payload.metadata.orderType.isPayOrder()). PAYEE orders fall into neither branch, so all storage, escrow, and hook logic is bypassed. The validateOrder function still returns success, validating the Seaport order.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (payload.metadata.orderType.isBaseOrder() || payload.metadata.orderType.isPayOrder()) { ... } — PAYEE orders skip entire block containing addRentals, increaseDeposit, and _addHooks

preconditions: A PAYEE order is fulfilled via Seaport
The paired PAY order doesn't exist, fails, or is stopped independently

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: PAYEE orders produce zero storage entries: no orders[hash], no rentedAssets, no balanceOf increment
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: PAYEE-transferred NFTs have no guard protection (not tracked as rented) and can be freely transferred by recipient
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Assets delivered via PAYEE fulfillment are effectively unrecoverable if PAY counterpart fails

economic_questions: Are PAYEE orders always atomic with their PAY counterpart in Seaport batch fulfillment?
Can a PAYEE order be fulfilled independently of its PAY counterpart?
What happens to PAYEE-side ERC20 payments if they're not tracked in escrow?
validation_requirements: Fulfill a PAYEE order independently and verify no storage entries exist
Attempt stopRent on a PAYEE order and verify failure
Check Seaport atomicity guarantees for PAY/PAYEE pairs

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Variable based on PAYEE order values

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: PAYEE order participants whose assets become untracked
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All PAYEE order values when PAY counterpart fails

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Seaport batch atomicity for PAY/PAYEE pairs
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm PAYEE orders produce zero storage footprint

validation_result.mechanism: Missing storage recording for PAYEE orders
validation_result.impact_summary: PAYEE orders pass full zone validation and return the success magic value to Seaport, but produce zero on-chain state: no Storage.orders entry, no rentedAssets tracking, no escrow deposit increment, no hooks, and no event emission. The code lacks any on-chain enforcement that PAYEE orders must be atomically paired with PAY orders.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The _rentFromZone function in Create.sol processes all order types through initial validation (metadata, safe owner, execution invariants, item conversion), but then gates ALL storage-recording logic behind the condition `payload.metadata.orderType.isBaseOrder() || payload.metadata.orderType.isPayOrder()`. Since PAYEE orders satisfy neither predicate, the entire block containing STORE.addRentals, ESCRW.increaseDeposit, _addHooks, and _emitRentalOrderStarted is skipped. The function then returns, and validateOrder returns the success magic value to Seaport. This means Seaport considers the PAYEE order fully validated and settled, but the protocol has zero record of it.

The design assumes PAYEE orders are always paired with PAY orders in atomic Seaport matchOrders calls, where the PAY order's storage recording covers all assets. However, there is no on-chain mechanism to enforce this invariant. Seaport can fulfill a PAYEE order independently (via fulfillOrder/fulfillAdvancedOrder), and the zone validation would succeed. In such a case, any NFTs sent to the rental safe would not be tracked in rentedAssets, meaning the Guard would not block their transfer. Any ERC20s sent to the escrow would not be reflected in balanceOf, making them appear as skimmable fee surplus.

While practical exploitation requires the protocol signer to have signed the PAYEE payload (which gates creation), and a rational attacker would not send their own assets to an untracked safe for no profit, the code defect is real: the protocol validates and approves an order without recording any state, violating the invariant that all validated orders should be trackable and stoppable.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The proof of signal is accurate. Lines 294-365 of Create.sol (approximately) show the _rentFromZone function with the explicit conditional block that excludes PAYEE orders from all storage operations. The condition at approximately line 316 (`if (payload.metadata.orderType.isBaseOrder() || payload.metadata.orderType.isPayOrder())`) creates a code path where PAYEE orders bypass addRentals, increaseDeposit, _addHooks, and event emission. The validateOrder function at lines ~435-486 still returns the success magic value regardless of order type.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: PAYEE orders pass all validation checks and the function returns normally, but no storage state is recorded. No order hash in Storage.orders, no rentedAssets updates, no escrow balanceOf increment, no hooks processed, and no event emitted. The protocol has zero on-chain awareness of the PAYEE order after validation.

validateOrder returns the success magic value for ALL order types including PAYEE, signaling to Seaport that the order is valid and should proceed, even though no protocol state was recorded for PAYEE orders.

calling_sequence: 1. Seaport calls Create.validateOrder with ZoneParameters for a PAYEE order
2. validateOrder decodes RentPayload and signature from zoneParams.extraData
10. _convertToItems processes as PAYEE: validates structure only, no Item population
11. Condition check: isBaseOrder() || isPayOrder() → FALSE for PAYEE
12. Function returns without entering the storage block
13. validateOrder returns success magic value to Seaport
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The vulnerability is in step 11-12: the conditional at Create.sol's _rentFromZone function excludes PAYEE orders from all storage operations (addRentals, increaseDeposit, _addHooks, event emission). After step 12, the function returns cleanly and step 13 returns success to Seaport. Seaport considers the order validated and all asset transfers finalized, but the protocol has no record of this order. If stopRent were called for the PAYEE order, it would fail because Storage.orders has no entry for it. Assets sent to the rental safe via PAYEE considerations have no rentedAssets tracking, so the Guard's isRentedOut check returns false and won't block transfers.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts contradict or are relevant to the PAYEE order processing logic. The DeployProtocol.s.sol script grants the SEAPORT role to the Seaport address and CREATE_SIGNER to a server-side signer, which are prerequisites for validateOrder to be called.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack scenario is partially valid. A PAYEE order can theoretically be fulfilled independently via Seaport (not through matchOrders but via fulfillOrder), provided the protocol signer has signed the payload. In this case, the fulfiller provides consideration items (NFTs to rental safe, ERC20s to escrow) while the PAYEE offerer receives nothing (no offers). The protocol validates and approves but records nothing. However, the practical exploitation is limited because: (1) the protocol signer must have signed the PAYEE payload, (2) independent PAYEE fulfillment requires the fulfiller to provide assets out of pocket with no direct economic benefit, and (3) the NFTs sent to the safe are the fulfiller's own assets. The more realistic concern is about desync between protocol state and actual asset positions if Seaport batch processing partially succeeds due to future Seaport changes.
validation_result.impact_assessment: MEDIUM severity. The code defect is real: PAYEE orders produce zero on-chain state while still being validated by the zone. This violates INV-STATE-009 which requires all validated orders to be trackable. Specific impacts: (1) NFTs sent to a rental safe via PAYEE considerations are not tracked in rentedAssets, so the Guard will not protect them from transfer; (2) ERC20s sent to escrow are not tracked in balanceOf, appearing as skimmable surplus rather than escrowed funds; (3) No stopRent mechanism exists for PAYEE orders since they have no Storage.orders entry. Practical exploitability is LOW due to protocol signer gatekeeping and lack of economic incentive for standalone PAYEE fulfillment.
recommended_mitigations: Add a check in _rentFromZone that validates PAYEE orders have a corresponding PAY order already recorded in Storage.orders (requires linking PAY and PAYEE orders, e.g., via shared Seaport order hash or a pairing ID in metadata)
Alternatively, record minimal storage for PAYEE orders (at minimum the order hash in Storage.orders) to maintain the invariant that all validated orders have on-chain footprint
Consider adding a revert for PAYEE orders that are not part of a Seaport batch (checking zoneParams context if available)

If PAYEE orders are truly never meant to have independent storage, add explicit documentation and consider whether validateOrder should only return success for PAYEE orders when specific batch conditions are met
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy the full protocol with Seaport integration. 2. Create a PAYEE order with valid metadata signed by the protocol signer. 3. Fulfill the PAYEE order independently (not matched with a PAY order) via Seaport's fulfillOrder. 4. Verify that validateOrder returns success. 5. Check Storage.orders for the PAYEE order hash — should be empty. 6. Check rentedAssets for any NFTs sent to the rental safe — should be zero. 7. Check PaymentEscrow.balanceOf for any ERC20s sent — should be zero. 8. Attempt to transfer NFTs out of the rental safe — should succeed since Guard sees no rental. 9. Attempt stopRent with PAYEE order data — should revert with OrderDoesNotExist.
uncertainties: Seaport's exact behavior when fulfilling a PAYEE-type order independently (no matching PAY order) — whether Seaport allows fulfillment of orders with zero offers
Whether the protocol signer would ever sign a PAYEE payload that could be used independently (operational controls may prevent this)
How totalExecutions in Seaport's zone callback reflects partial vs full batch processing
additional_testing_needed: Test independent PAYEE order fulfillment via Seaport to verify whether Seaport permits this
Verify that PAYEE order fulfillment results in zero Storage entries
Test Guard behavior for NFTs in a safe that arrived via untracked PAYEE fulfillment
Test whether escrow balanceOf desync occurs when ERC20s arrive via PAYEE fulfillment
